comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
The solution writes the finite free convolution as

  p(x) ⊞_n q(x) = Σ_{π∈S_n} Π_{i=1}^n (x − α_i − β_{π(i)})

The right-hand side has leading coefficient n! (one contribution per permutation), so it is NOT monic. This contradicts the problem statement, which defines p⊞_nq as a monic polynomial with c_0=1.

The correct formula (consistent with the Marcus–Spielman–Srivastava coefficient definition c_k = Σ_{i+j=k} (n−i)!(n−j)!/(n!(n−k)!) a_i b_j) is:

  p(x) ⊞_n q(x) = (1/n!) Σ_{π∈S_n} Π_{i=1}^n (x − α_i − β_{π(i)})

For the purposes of the proof, the roots (and hence Φ_n, hyperbolicity, and the Jacobian) are unchanged by the scalar factor n!, so no downstream step is invalidated. However, the formula as stated is mathematically incorrect and conflicts with the monic normalisation used throughout the proof and problem statement.

comments.author: system
comments.body:
**Proof file:** `webapp/issues/q4/working_solution.tex`

The issue described above occurs in this working solution file. Reviewers should consult the file at that path for the exact context.

comments.author: solver-agent
comments.body:
**Issue resolved**: Missing 1/n! normalization factor in Walsh formula (eq:walsh)

## Analysis
The finite free convolution must be a monic polynomial by definition (see problem.tex, coefficients formula with c_0 = a_0 b_0 = 1). The previous formula:

p(x) ⊞_n q(x) = Σ_{π∈S_n} Π_{i=1}^n (x − α_i − β_{π(i)})

has leading coefficient n! (one contribution per permutation π), making it non-monic and inconsistent with the Marcus–Spielman–Srivastava definition.

## Fix Applied
1. **eq:walsh (line 63)**: Added the 1/n! normalization factor:
   p(x) ⊞_n q(x) = (1/n!) Σ_{π∈S_n} Π_{i=1}^n (x − α_i − β_{π(i)})

2. **Corollary proof (line 271)**: Updated the polynomial f to include the same 1/n! factor for consistency with eq:walsh.

## Why This Is Safe
The scalar factor 1/n! does not affect:
- The **roots** of the polynomial (only the coefficients scale)
- The **moments** and **variance** (extracted via ratios of coefficients, where n! cancels)
- The **hyperbolicity** property (preserved under scalar multiplication by Walsh's theorem)
- The **Jacobian analysis** and downstream PSD arguments (all depend on roots, not coefficients)

This was the key observation in the original criticism: the mathematics downstream is unchanged, but the formula is now mathematically correct and consistent with the problem statement.

## Verification
- Proposition 1 (additivity): Newton identities on the normalized formula yield the same mean and variance additivity properties
- Corollary 1 (convexity): BGLS theorem applies to the normalized polynomial with preserved hyperbolicity
- Overall proof structure: Unchanged, as all subsequent steps depend on roots and moments, not the leading coefficient
